content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 10,
1994
Honorable Roger Zwanziger
Board of Supervisors, Siskiyou County
Post Office Box 338
Yreka, California
96097
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-157
Dear Supervisor Zwanziger:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
regarding your responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPCOps.
71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
May Supervisor Zwanziger participate in the decision
whether to site a correctional facility if he and the City of Weed
modify their existing contract to cap the supply of effluent
available to Supervisor Zwanziger?
2.
May Supervisor Zwanziger participate in decisions
concerning the siting of the correctional facility if the people
of Siskiyou County approve a measure allowing the siting of the
correctional facility in Siskiyou County?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
The contract with the City of Weed will not cause a
conflict of interest for Supervisor Zwanziger with regard to the
siting of the correctional facility, if it is modified to cap the
effluent available to him through the sewer main,
pipe and
pipeline at the present level.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—94--157
Page
2
2.
We do not have enough information to give an answer to
this question.
Generally, Supervisor Zwanziger may participate in
implementing decisions concerning the siting of the correctional
facility if the decision in question will not have a foreseeable
and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally,
on him or his economic interests.
FACTS
Supervisor Zwanziger is a member of the board of supervisors
of the County of Siskiyou.
Election to the board of supervisors
is by district and Supervisor Zwanziger’s district includes the
City of Weed.
The Department of Corrections is considering several sites in
or around the City of Weed for locating a correctional facility.
The Weed City Council has approved such action and now approval is
being sought from the Siskiyou County Board of Supervisors.
In 1977,
Supervisor Zwanziger and his wife entered into an
agreement with the City of Weed.
This agreement transfers certain
lands owned by the Zwanzigers to the City of Weed for operation of
its sewer system and,
in return, makes available to the Zwanzigers
through a sewer main, pipe and pipeline a guaranteed minimum
amount of effluent suitable for irrigation purposes for a specific
period of time during the year.
The agreement also makes excess
water,
above the guaranteed minimum, available to the Zwanzigers;
thus, the amount of excess water available to the Zwanzigers
increases as additional sewage becomes available.
Such water
provides a stable irrigation source for lands owned by the
Zwanzigers,
enhances the value of such lands,
and is exclusive to
the Zwanzigers’
lands.
At the time the contract was signed, the guaranteed minimum
was 450 gallons per minute of effluent of a quality suitable for
forage crop irrigation.
The system is currently capable of
producing 1,250 gallons per minute, entitling Supervisor Zwanziger
to 625 gallons per minute
(50 percent).
It is expected that the
siting of a correctional facility or other development around the
City of Weed would increase the amount of overflow available, thus
benefiting Supervisor Zwanziger’s lands.
The county counsel has concluded that the decision to site
the correctional facility in Supervisor Zwanziger’s district will
have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on
Supervisor Zwanziger.
Supervisor Zwanziger is attempting to
determine whether a modification of his contract with the City of
Weed,
capping the effluent that is available to him at the current
level, will resolve the conflict of interest caused by the
contract.
The system is presently capable of producing 1,250 gallons
per minute; thus, it is proposed that the contract be modified to
cap the amount of effluent available to Supervisor Zwanziger


File No.
A—94—157
Page
3
through the sewer main, pipe and pipeline at 50 percent of the
amount currently available or
625 gallons per minute.
Siskiyou County has a population of approximately 43,000
persons.
As stated above, elections to this board of supervisors
is by district and the population of Supervisor Zwanziger’s
district is approximately 5,500; this includes the City of Weed
which has a population of 3,180 persons.
ANALYSIS
1.
May Supervisor Zwanziger Participate in the Decision to
Site A Correctional Facility Followinc the Modification
of His Contract with the City of Weed?
Section 87100 provides that no public official at any level
of state or local government shall make, participate in making,
or
in any way attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
oron:
*
*
*
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
Section 87103(b).
Supervisor Zwanziger and the county counsel have concluded
that the present contract between the Zwanzigers and the City of
Weed will result in Supervisor Zwanziger’s having a conflict of
interest.
Therefore, this analysis does not address that
threshold issue.
Rather, the narrow issue considered here is
whether the proposed modification of his contract with the City of
Weed, capping the effluent available to Supervisor Zwanziger at 50
percent of the amount currently available,
or 625 gallons per
minute, will remove the contract as a potential conflict for


File No.
A—94—157
Page
4
Supervisor Zwanziger.
2
If the amount of effluent made available through the sewer
main, pipe and pipeline is capped at 625 gallons per minute, then,
despite the potential increase of overflow resulting from the
siting of the correctional facility, the amount of effluent
available to the Zwanzigers will not increase and cannot benefit
Supervisor Zwanziger.
Accordingly, it is our opinion that such
modification will remove his rights under the contract as a
conflict.
3
2.
Participation in Decisions Following an Affirmative Vote
of the People of Siskiyou County.
Regulation 18700
(copy enclosed) provides in relevant part:
(d)
Making or participating in the making of
a governmental decision shall not include:
(1)
Actions of public officials which are
solely ministerial, secretarial,
manual or clerical
Supervisor Zwanziger has asked whether he may participate in
decisions concerning the siting of the correctional facility if
the people of Siskiyou County approve a measure in November
allowing the siting of the correctional facility in Siskiyou
2
The water supplied by the City of Weed to Supervisor Zwanziger
is deemed to be an in—kind payment to Supervisor Zwanziger;
therefore, the City of Weed is a “source of income” to Supervisor
Zwanziger within the meaning of Section 87103(c)
and a potentially
disqualifying conflict of interest.
However, since any benefit
flowing to the City of Weed will flow to its residents,
and since
the population of the City of Weed is 3,180 and the population of
Supervisor Zwanziger’s district is 5,500, pursuant to Regulation
18703(a) (1) (A) Ci) the effect will be the same as the effect on the
public generally.
Accordingly, this economic interest does not
disqualify Supervisor Zwanziger from participating in the decision
to site the correctional facility in his district.
The conclusion reached herein is based solely on Supervisor
Zwanziger’s economic interest resulting from his contract with the
City of Weed providing effluent for Supervisor Zwanziger’s lands
through the sewer main, pipe and pipeline.
It does not focus on
any potential conflicts resulting from any other economic
interests, including his real property interests, which may arise
from the decision to site the correctional facility in Supervisor
Zwanz iger’ s district.


File No.
A—94—157
Page
5
County.
4
As stated in Regulation 18700, subdivision
(d) (1), making or
participating in the making of a governmental decision does not
include actions by public officials which are solely ministerial.
Accordingly,
any ministerial decisions concerning the siting of
the correctional facility following approval of the measure will
not constitute “making or participating in the making of a
governmental decision.”
Nevertheless,
each of the implementing
decisions must be analyzed independently to determine whether the
decision involves the exercise of discretion by Supervisor
Zwanziger.
If so,
he must determine whether the decision will
have a foreseeable and material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally,
on Supervisor Zwanziger
or one of his economic interests.
We would be glad to assist you
in analyzing these matters as they arise.
I hope the above analysis has answered your questions
concerning the siting of the correctional facility in your
district.
Should you have any additional questions regarding this
matter, please feel free to contact me at 916/322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By
Jeevan Ahuja
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:JA:ak
Please note that if Supervisor Zwanziger is disqualified from
participating in the decision to site the correctional facility,
he also would be disqualified from participating in the decision
to place the issue before the voters as a’ballot measure.
(See
Skousen Advice Letter,
No. A-88-162; Larsen Advice Letter,
No. A
87—164;
and Beniamin Advice Letter,
No.
A—86-061
(copies
enclosed).)

this question.

implementing decisions concerning the siting of the correctional
facility if the decision in question will not have a foreseeable
and material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally,
on him or his economic interests.
